python.explanation:
The correlation between two variables X_j and Y_k is defined as their covariance divided by the product of their standard deviations: r(X_j, Y_k) = cov(X_j, Y_k) / (σ_Xj · σ_Yk). This normalisation maps the value into [-1, 1], where ±1 means perfect linear relationship and 0 means no linear relationship. The implementation first computes column-wise means, then population standard deviations, and finally fills a 2-D list by evaluating the Pearson formula for every pair of columns.

When Y is omitted, it defaults to X, producing the standard auto-correlation (or more precisely, the Pearson correlation matrix) of the dataset with itself. In this case the diagonal entries are always 1.0 because each variable is perfectly correlated with itself. A zero-standard-deviation guard prevents division-by-zero for constant columns, returning 0 in that edge case.

The triple nested loop makes the time complexity O(n · p · q) where n is the number of rows, p the number of columns in X, and q the number of columns in Y. This is the expected complexity given that every cell of the output requires a full pass over the n samples.
key_learnings: Pearson correlation normalises covariance by the product of standard deviations, bounding the result to [-1, 1] regardless of feature scale — this is why feature scaling is not needed before computing correlation.
The correlation matrix is symmetric (and equals the identity on the diagonal) only for the auto-correlation case X vs X; the cross-correlation matrix X vs Y is generally not square or symmetric.
Using population std (÷n) vs sample std (÷n-1) cancels out in the final ratio, so both conventions give the same correlation value.
Always guard against zero standard deviation (constant columns); failing to do so causes a division-by-zero that can silently propagate NaN through downstream computations.
In interviews, be ready to derive the formula from scratch: start from the definition of covariance, then explain why dividing by σ_X · σ_Y makes the metric scale-invariant.

numpy.code:
import numpy as np

def calculate_correlation_matrix(X, Y=None):
    if Y is None:
        Y = X

    n = X.shape[0]

    # Center each column (subtract its mean)
    X_c = X - X.mean(axis=0)          # shape (n, p)
    Y_c = Y - Y.mean(axis=0)          # shape (n, q)

    # Population standard deviations for each column
    X_std = X.std(axis=0)             # shape (p,)
    Y_std = Y.std(axis=0)             # shape (q,)

    # Covariance matrix via matrix multiplication: (p, n) @ (n, q) -> (p, q)
    cov_matrix = (X_c.T @ Y_c) / n

    # Outer product of std vectors gives the denominator matrix
    denom = np.outer(X_std, Y_std)    # shape (p, q)

    # Avoid division by zero for constant columns
    denom = np.where(denom == 0, 1.0, denom)

    corr_matrix = cov_matrix / denom

    return corr_matrix


# ── Verification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == '__main__':
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    X = rng.standard_normal((100, 4))

    # Auto-correlation should match np.corrcoef
    our_result  = calculate_correlation_matrix(X)
    ref_result  = np.corrcoef(X, rowvar=False)
    print('Max deviation from np.corrcoef:', np.max(np.abs(our_result - ref_result)))

    # Cross-correlation example
    Y = rng.standard_normal((100, 3))
    cross = calculate_correlation_matrix(X, Y)
    print('Cross-correlation shape:', cross.shape)   # (4, 3)
numpy.explanation:
The key insight is that the full p×q covariance matrix can be computed in one shot as (1/n) · X_cᵀ Y_c, exploiting BLAS-level matrix multiplication instead of explicit loops. Centering (subtracting the column mean) is broadcast-vectorised across all n rows simultaneously. The result is then element-wise divided by np.outer(σ_X, σ_Y), which constructs the denominator matrix where entry [j, k] equals σ_Xj · σ_Yk.

np.where replaces any zero denominator with 1.0 before the division, keeping the corresponding correlation at 0 rather than NaN. This is numerically safer than adding a small epsilon because it exactly zeros the output for constant columns rather than introducing a tiny non-zero artefact.

For the auto-correlation case (Y = X), this implementation matches np.corrcoef(X, rowvar=False) exactly. np.corrcoef internally uses np.cov, which divides by n-1 (Bessel's correction), but because the same correction factor appears in both the numerator (cov) and denominator (std²), it cancels and both conventions yield identical correlation values.
key_learnings: Expressing the covariance matrix as a single matrix product X_cᵀ Y_c / n is both faster (O(n·p·q) in optimised BLAS) and simpler to read than nested loops — a pattern that recurs in PCA, linear regression, and attention mechanisms.
np.outer(a, b) constructs the rank-1 matrix where entry [i,j] = a[i]·b[j]; it is the canonical NumPy idiom for computing all pairwise products of two 1-D arrays.
Population std (ddof=0, the default) and sample std (ddof=1) give identical Pearson correlation values because the correction factor cancels in the normalisation step.

np.corrcoef is a convenient built-in for the auto-correlation case, but it does not directly support cross-correlation between two separate matrices — knowing the manual derivation lets you generalise it.
Broadcasting rules: subtracting a shape (p,) mean from a shape (n, p) matrix works automatically along axis=0; always verify axis semantics (axis=0 operates down rows, axis=1 across columns).
